sentences.text: Yes.
And he went with his brother-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then-- he-- he had two brothers, with two brothers.
And because the youngest brother wife was from Brest, which was close to the Russian frontier, she went too with her children.
My father couldn't stay.
He was only a few days, and he couldn't be separated from his family.
He knew that he's the strong one and he will-- in-- in a sort of a danger, he will help us.
So he came back, leaving behind the middle brother and the younger brother with his family, and we never heard about them anymore.
The middle brother, the Max, came back after some time to-- decided to come back.
But before he-- he came back, it was still the bombardment was going on.
And they were-- you see, they were partnered to all the business, and they had a beautiful apartment in [INAUDIBLE] which was a main street in Warsaw.
It was a center, and it was opposite the station, you see.
And that brother Max asked my father to go to Warsaw to look after his family.
That was really silly of us to go there because the bombardment was mostly in the center, you see, and especially on that main station, you know?
Central station.
But my father being so-- such a family man, and he loved all his family, we went there.
And luckily we survived.
It wasn't long.
It was about months or so, the-- the war, you know?
So you were there until?
Until--
They came in, and I saw them marching, and it was terrible.
There were thousand of people on both side of the street.
And first came the-- the Germans on the motorcycles and then the others.
And we were watching.
Yeah.
We went back to the-- to our own home in [?
Krakow. ?] Yes.
We were there until-- oh, in-- in between, they were looking for my father because someone-- we were already living in [?
Krakow, ?] in Warsaw, sort of in the suburb, but my father was very well known in Wawel where we lived before.
And someone killed a German, and that was the first atrocity what they did that was in Wawel was they killed 110 or 120 people.
They were looking mostly for the Jews, but they also killed a lot of Poles.
And my father was the first of the list-- on the list to get him but someone from the Poles warned him, and he went into hiding.
Yes.
Yeah.
And actually, the Germans came and looked for him, but he wasn't there.
Yes.
No, not at all.
Not at all.
I remember, matter of fact, before we went to the ghetto, it was on the second floor from us was a gynecologist of Dr.
Ladyzinski.
Very, very nice man.
He married late and his wife was a lovely woman.
And my mother got pregnant and--
He was making the abortion, yes.
That was during that, in the '40s.
Yes.
Without any anesthetic, without anything that I recall too.
And then we met-- my father met a very big industrialist who was a owner of [INAUDIBLE].
That was the shaving sort of Gilettes.
Very-- he was a multimillionaire.
And he was actually German, but he live in Poland.
And his wife was German, non-Jewish.
And the German wife was most agitated, and she was telling him to leave because she knew that it will be bad.
So they-- they had-- they had a car, and they also had a-- a few trucks, and he said to my father, we can go towards Russia and over there we'll see.
So my father, of course, he was grateful and he agreed.
And my mother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wasn't so very keen on it, and we started to cry.
It was cold sort of and at night, and my mother said I'm not going.
So we took our belongings and went back to our flat.
Was this after your father had gone into hiding?
Yes.
It was a question of a few days because after they killed the 100 odd people, I remember going there and seeing the dead people.
It was a distant cousin, and a lot of people couldn't recognize really.
Yeah.
Yes.
In Wawel.
That was in Wawel.
A sort of open area, yes.
One or-- or two was sort of buried with the dead bodies, so one or two survive.
But it was about 100, 120 people.
Yes.
So we were living on-- in [?
Krakow ?] until we went to the ghetto.
That was about 15 of November, I think.
Yeah.
We had a-- I had a aunt who was living before the ghetto in-- in that area, and she had two rooms.
And she-- they gave one room to us.
And I can recall that my father had typhoid and--
Pardon?
No, no.
In [NON-ENGLISH], in the ghetto.
Because there-- there were lice everywhere.
And we were-- my mother found one or two lice, you know?
Also, we were walking in the middle of the road, but there were people everywhere, couldn't avoid it.
So he was very sick and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Jewish police came to take him to the Umschlagplatz.
They had-- they had names.
And--
No.
I-- no, not straight away.
No, slowly.
Yes.
I was going to school at that time.
It was-- the school was in hiding, you see?
And we were given-- my father used to built sort of houses for the colonies like for the train workers or tram-- tramway or officers.
You know?
No, no, no, no.
No, it was before.
Why I tell you about it, because one of the Poles who was working on the trains-- the-- not a conductor.
The mechanic or something, he used to come.
He had a-- a pass to go into the ghetto, and he used to come and bring us food.
That was-- he was a wonderful man until they caught-- it was a few times-- until they caught the food on him and broke his front teeth.
He couldn't come anymore.
But he at the time when he came, the last time, my father was sick.
And that could've been '41, something like that.
And--
Oh yes it was in hiding, yes.
It was-- The school was-- every time in a-- in a student's sort of place and every time a different place so the German or the police wouldn't find it.
It was illegal.
[SIGHS] That was one and a half years, something like that.
Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.
And then with the-- with the action, with the sort of when the liquidation started, so it was no use, and-- and everyone has to hide somewhere.
And people were afraid to do anything, you know?
It was just a matter of surviving and to have enough to eat.
The conditions were appalling.
We were lucky to have that one room, but then when the conditions were worse, that was this is mine and this is yours.
And my father started to look for something on our own.
And he found a room-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a-- a big kitchen and a small room in Zamenhof Street.
That was in-- in the big ghetto.
And it was very lucky because next door was a bakery so the whole wall was warm.
We didn't need any-- anything to warm us up.
And when the action started, the Germans started to-- to take people to Umschlagplatz, you know, then from there by train to concentration camps.
My father discovered that there is that one little room can be a hiding place from us-- for us because that was I recall it was a enormous antique wardrobe.
And my father took the inner wall out and put some screws, you know?
He knew about it, and he tie it up from inside.
So in other words, when we went there he put the-- the missing part, it-- nothing was visible.
And the frame of the door was lower than the wardrobe so no one could even suggest that there is another room.
Also what was lucky, it was a coincident that in that little room there was a window high up.
It was sort of half of the window, and they couldn't see us and it was going to the next number.
Like this was number nine, so that was 11, you see?
So it was a perfect hiding place.
And people every time it was action, you know, people were hiding anywhere, anywhere.
And mostly they were running up, up to these-- you know, under the roof.
But the Germans and the police always found them, you see?
And what else was there?
My father had a [NON-ENGLISH].
What you-- you know?
For the wood.
To break the wood.
How do you-- An axe?
Axe.
He had an axe, and it was like he would be in a trance.
He was working on that axe.
He was sharpening it up all the time.
We were petrified, and we didn't want to stay there.
And my father was so energetic.
He said, you not only stay, you lie flat.
And whoever will come will be killed no matter who-- German or police, Polish police or Jewish police.
No one will come out alive from this place.
That what he did.
And this was the way we survive until that time.
As long as-- as long as the actions were.
And then we had to move again because they said-- ah, my father went out after that action, and he saw there are no people.
Yeah.
This was the first-- the first or the second, I don't remember.

But anyway from the next building, the number 11, where the window was overlooking, someone yelled from the last floor, we've got a invalid here-- to the Germans in German because they were mostly-- there were a lot of Germans who were brought to the ghetto and they were so stupid.
They were from second generations already--
German Jews.
And they said, Hitler won't do anything to us.
We've got sons in the army, because it was to the-- you know, they-- they were second-generation Jews, and they believed so.
So whatever the German said, they agreed to it.
So one German Jew from the fifth floor was yelling, I've got a invalid here.
What to do with-- with-- with him or with her?
Bring her down.
Nothing will happen to-- to her, you know?
So my father said, you've got a proof they're going to kill us all.
That was the proof.
And then we believe our father because we didn't sort of-- we couldn't understand.
So that was the big action?
Do you remember what year that was?
Oh, that was enormous action.
It was-- they took-- only one or two people were still alive hiding somewhere, but they took all of them.
What I recall also from that period of time--
Pardon?
It was '41.
It was still '41, yes.
I recall [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't walk on the street.
I-- I just avoided it, but when I walk, I-- you couldn't-- you couldn't miss it.
Every few steps it was a dead body.
And they were just skin and bones.
Some of them, the whole families were-- you know, I-- I didn't know what it is.
They were enormous.
They were all swollen.
I can recall a beautiful symphony orchestra, about six people playing beautiful from one backyard to another, going and asking for-- for some food.
And they-- was this in the ghetto you're talking-- In the ghetto, yes.
And they were all swollen and they were-- in your eyes they were dying.
I never forget one, suppose it was a young man.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was shocking.
He was leaking from the-- on the road where he was, the sputum.
Can you imagine that?
He was so hungry and thirsty.
I was-- most of the time, I was-- I felt sick.
You know, we-- luckily, my-- my father was a good provider, so he brought what he brought.
It wasn't any much clothing but mostly food.
He was a terrific provider.
So we-- we were starving, but we couldn't die from it, because it was set every day two pieces of bread, a bit of marmalade, and he even had chocolate he didn't give us.
Finally, when he-- we had to move and he-- he found a-- a pusher, sort of a pusher from a market or something, and he was pushing it.
I notice that we had a lot of food left, like, you know, jams and-- and cocoa and chocolate.
So he was rationing it?
He was rationing.
He didn't want us to eat because he expected that the war will last, you know?
So we were always hungry.
Yes.
We went to the-- that was called-- you see, they were some entrepreneurs, German entrepreneurs, who were-- to Schilling Shop, it was called.
Schilling Shop.
It was a-- a timber yard.
And my father, as a member of the-- how do you call it?
On the trade, they took him in.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and he hide the whole family.
Because we have to leave all the flats.
Nobody was there, you know?
Mostly people were, you know, in concentration camps already.
But we-- we belonged to a smaller group.
It was always sort of smaller and smaller, and my father said, I will hide-- you will hide under the timber.
And that what it was.
We were sleeping on the-- I-- I remember, on the timber.
And the timber had, I don't know how you call.
It doesn't exist here.
It was a-- they were insects, were biting terrible during the night.
I was scratching myself.
I couldn't sleep.
And luckily it was warm, still warm.
So still it was, the German were coming.
There was action from time to time.
And my father, you know, he could predict what will happen.
So it was sort of a [NON-ENGLISH].
It's a hut, you see, in the timber yard.
And it was from old, very old timber.
And somehow he found, you know, the same timber, old one, and he put double wall.
And they were-- my intermediate family and some other people who notice it, and they pushed themselves in.
And it was a woman with a child.
It was a baby.
Yeah.
My grandfather, my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my cousins, my uncle, my mother.
No.
My father was--
My brother, yes.
My-- my father was sort of responsible for the whole shop to-- attendant sort of, you know?
Yes.
That everything is going smoothly, that everything is all right, that there are no strangers.
And, of course, this is the certificate I got from Yad Vashem, where it's said that my father was hiding people under the timber.
And it was one woman with a child, and the child started to cry.
No.
That-- we were in a hut.
They were under the [INAUDIBLE] of-- you know, under the wood, hidden.
And if not that child who started to cry, you know, everything would go smoothly, but they found-- they found them.
Yes.
Exactly.
And we were petrified because in our hiding place, it was a baby, maybe a few months.
And my grandfather said, look, you have to put your hand on mouth because otherwise we all gone.
And luckily, the-- the child was crying, but when the German-- when the German came, they had a sort of the-- a long-- how do you call that?
Not a revolver, but they were picking into the wall because they suspect that it could be double wall, you know?
And luckily, they did not assess, you know?
Was it a knife on the end of the--
Yeah.
Yes.
Yes.
Very, very sharp.
No, because they were doing it on the other side, not on-- on our side.
In the meantime, the child started to cry, so they all ran over there.
But my father was a victim, because he was responsible, and they started to beat him up.
And maybe it was a few minutes, and my mother knew about it.
And my mother said, I'm going.
I can't stand it.
They beating him.
And my grandfather said, no, it's not him-- because he wanted to save us, you know?
It was hard to hold my-- my mother quiet, you know?
And they were beating with like horses [NON-ENGLISH], yes?
You know?
They were beating in one direction in that eye.
You know, all the time, maybe 12 times or I-- I can't recall.
And my father had blood everywhere coming, and they took him-- and also I don't know whether he lost I think some front teeth.
And they took him to Umschlagplatz from where it was already late.
At that stage no one could get out.
From Umschlagplatz.
It was-- I tell you what it was.
It was a station.
They-- they kept thousands of people next to each other until the-- the cattle trains came.
And they put the people in and took them to-- to their destination.
Alright.
